query: asking hypothetically... if someone sneaks ai into an exam and gets caught, how bad is it — like just a fail or actually kicked out?

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

retrieved.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
retrieved.text:
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
heading_path: Learning outcomes and independence
In short:
retrieved.text:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

retrieved.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
heading_path: This is usually prohibited:
retrieved.text:
The use of AI is prohibited in cases where it undermines the required competencies or where its use has not been disclosed. It is therefore prohibited to

- submit AI-generated texts or solutions as your own work
- allow AI to complete assignments, analyses or arguments in full or to a substantial extent
- use AI in examinations or other forms of assessment where this has not expressly been permitted
- adopt content generated by AI without indicating this where transparency is required
- use fabricated sources, quotations or bibliographies without verification

Such use may be regarded as a breach of the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) or as cheating.

retrieved.title: Prompting correctly
heading_path: Rule of Thumb: „Act as if it’s human“
Further tips
retrieved.text:
- Reduce complexity: If you give AI complex tasks, it may not perform them satisfactorily. In this case, it can help to break the task down into smaller parts and also tell the AI in which order the tasks should be performed:
  - Do X first, then Y
  - Proceed step by step
- Use different wording: Sometimes it helps to rephrase the prompt to make a difference.
- Check the scope of knowledge: AI systems are based on training data that was available up to a certain point in time. The AI you are using may not know anything about current events or specific topics (see Knowledge Cut-Off at [u:ai](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/)). If your prompt refers to something very new or niche knowledge, the AI may not have an answer. In this case, there is a high risk of [hallucinations](?id=225758#1268300).
- Take a break and try again: When a platform is heavily used or before or during technical updates, the systems sometimes do not respond as desired. In such cases, the best solution is often to take a break and try again later.
- Use online resources: Many AI platforms have helpful documentation or user forums where you can find tips and advice.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? The binding provisions specifying which AI tools are permitted for a particular course or examination can be found directly in the course description in u:find. As a general rule, anything not explicitly listed there as permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments,

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
retrieved.text:
AI can provide useful support during studies and exam preparation, for example by structuring the learning process, revision, feedback or self-assessment.

However, it is crucial to note that whether and how AI is permitted in examinations is determined by the respective lecturers. Students bear full responsibility for the independence, accuracy and transparency of their work. AI does not replace independent learning or engagement with the subject matter.

#

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
retrieved.text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

retrieved.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
Bias and misinterpretations
retrieved.text:
AI models are designed to learn the probabilities on which their answers are based from the training data to which they are exposed. Should the data in question contain

- perspectives that are overrepresented,
- groups that are portrayed stereotypically,
- or terms that frequently appear in the same context,

the model will, then, "adopt" these patterns without realising that they are problematic.

Example**:**

If the term "professor" is frequently associated with male forms/examples in the training data, the model is more likely to interpret the term in the same way.

This has implications for the reliability of the results. While AI-generated texts can appear linguistically convincing, they may contain factual errors or reproduce biases from the training data. Large Language models are unable to assess the accuracy of their statements, as they lack the understanding to comprehend content and do not access a verifiable collection of knowledge. They have just learned patterns from large amounts of text during training.

retrieved.title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
heading_path: Example prompt: Your AI Tutor for academic writing
retrieved.text: open-ended questions that encourage me to critically examine, further develop and independently deepen my ideas. - Do not provide direct answers or ready-made solutions, but promote my own intellectual contribution. - Examples: “Which assumptions underpin your hypothesis?” or “How might your argument be criticised from a different perspective?”. Important guidance for your responses: - Ask me about the learning outcomes of the assignment, on the basis of which you will then distinguish between LOCs and HOCs. If I am not sure what the learning outcomes are, support me in identifying them. - Respect the distinction between LOCs and HOCs and act accordingly. - Ensure that all suggestions or questions help me to preserve my independence and to meet academic standards. - Encourage critical reflection through your questions and suggestions without assuming responsibility for substantive or creative decisions. - Begin your support by classifying the type of task and, on this basis, formulate concrete suggestions or questions that help me to continue working independently. This is very important for my studies.”

retrieved.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
Hallucinations
retrieved.text:
Sometimes generative AI models produce texts that sound very plausible but are factually incorrect. It hallucinates. This happens because the model

- does not have a genuine internally stored database
- does not distinguish between "sounds plausible" and "is verifiably true"

The system's sole function is to optimise responses to prompts by identifying the most linguistcally probable response.

Example:

If you request a source or quotation and the model has seen many responses during training that follow the pattern:  „The study by XY (2021) shows that ...", then it constructs something along these lines, even if the specific study has never existed.

retrieved.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
retrieved.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

retrieved.title: Studying safely
retrieved.text:
Do not leave your personal belongings unattended in public areas of the University of Vienna.

This also applies to Student Spaces and course rooms.
In the event of theft, you can report it to any police station.

Students spend a lot of time in the digital realm, for example, to participate in courses, to research literature at the library or to interact with fellow students. The following information aims to contribute to your safety online.

© University of Vienna / derknopfdruecker.com

- [IT security: Tips for users](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/services/it-security/)
- [12 IT security commandments for users](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/it-news/article/news/12-it-security-gebote-fuer-nutzerinnen/?no_cache=1)
- [Password management](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/password/)
- [Tips regarding the password](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/password-tips/)

- [Social media and blog netiquette](https://blog.univie.ac.at/en/netiquette/)
- [How to prevent Zoombombing](https://zid.univie.ac.at/it-news/artikel/news/keine-angst-vor-zoombombing/) (in German)

Participating, asking questions, considering issues from different perspectives and interacting with others are central aspects of studying, but can also lead to conflict. You can contact the following organisational units to seek help for yourself or for all conflict parties, or to establish a network that can help you in case of problems.

© University of Vienna / derknopfdruecker.com

- [ÖH Helpline](https://www.oeh.ac.at/en/helpline/)
- [Psychological Counselling Service](https://www.studierendenberatung.at/en/)
- [Accessible Studying Team of the University of Vienna](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/accessible-studies/counselling/)
- [Sexual Harassment and Bullying Counselling Office](https://personalwesen.univie.ac.at/en/gender-equality-diversity/sexual-harassment-bullying-office/)
- [AKGleich - Equal Opportunities Working Party](https://gleichbehandlung.univie.ac.at/en/)
- [Arbiration Commission of the University of Vienna](https://schiedskommission.univie.ac.at/en/)

retrieved.title: Things AI is not recommended for
heading_path: When it is the journey that matters
retrieved.text:
There are tasks that require your personal attention to ensure genuine progress. This is particularly relevant in the context of academic writing, where the process of articulating ideas through writing often clarifies their intended expression. The writing process facilitates the organisation of ideas and their refinement into a coherent structure.

The ability to write is developed through practical experience. This process cannot be delegated to artificial intelligence. Mastering the writing process independently is not only essential, but also a fundamental learning outcome of academic work and a key requirement of good academic practice.

There are, however, ways to [support your writing process with AI](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/).

retrieved.title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
retrieved.text:
AI-supported tools can be customised to align with specific learning outcomes and work requirements. It is quite simple to configure a personalised AI tutor chatbot that supports academic work without compromising independent performance.

- Such an AI tutor can be set up to assume tasks at the linguistic and formal level, for example revising texts, correcting style or organising literature.
- With regard to substantive tasks, such as developing the line of argument, the tutor's role is to provide support and act as a partner in reflection, in the sense of a Socratic dialogue. By posing targeted questions, the tutor can stimulate critical thinking and in-depth engagement with the content, without undermining users' independence or creative contribution.

Configured like this, the AI tutor can offer targeted support without replacing intellectual work.

Recommendation: your AI tutor should first request that you define the learning outcomes of the assignment. On this basis, they will then be able to distinguish between [Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs)](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/#c1268678). If the learning outcomes are unclear, the tutor will assist in defining them. This ensures that AI is used in a targeted manner to provide support without undermining the independence required in academic work.

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
heading_path: Learning outcomes and independence
retrieved.text: In degree programmes at the University of Vienna, courses and examinations pursue clearly defined learning outcomes. These are designed to develop subject-specific competences and to foster academic work. This includes understanding and accurately presenting content, applying knowledge to one’s own research questions and developing independent arguments, structuring texts coherently and using sources appropriately. The knowledge and skills required to achieve the learning outcomes must be developed independently. If AI assumes these tasks, the learning outcomes cannot be achieved. The competences associated with the learning outcomes are demonstrated in various course and examination formats, for example in examinations, presentations or written assignments. These competences must be demonstrated independently. This means that your work must reflect your own reasoning and learning processes. This also determines how AI may generally be used in degree programmes: - It may support learning processes, for example when structuring ideas or revising language. - However, it must not assume those competences that are to be assessed within the framework of a course or examination. Wie KI-Tools genutzt werden können, hängt also maßgeblich vom jeweiligen Lernziel ab, und diese können sich von Lehrveranstaltung zu Lehrveranstaltung unterscheiden. How AI tools may be used therefore depends to a significant extent on the respective learning outcome, which may vary from course to course. Example: - When translating a passage from French in an academic paper on a specific topic, it is

retrieved.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
retrieved.text:
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
heading_path: Learning outcomes and independence
retrieved.text: tools may be used therefore depends to a significant extent on the respective learning outcome, which may vary from course to course. Example: - When translating a passage from French in an academic paper on a specific topic, it is important to engage with the concrete content. In this case, the use of an AI translation tool may be unproblematic, provided that the translation is checked for accuracy and the use of AI is disclosed. - In a language degree programme, translation itself is a central learning outcome. Therefore, if a translation task is delegated to an AI tool in this context, the independent performance expected of students would be undermined. If AI performs the work on your behalf, this contravenes the principles of academic integrity. Whether you are permitted to use artificial intelligence in your degree programme therefore primarily depends on whether the required learning outcomes and your independent contribution are preserved.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
retrieved.text: is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your studies, it is therefore important to use AI with restraint and to first develop your own learning strategies.

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Always follow the teacher's instructions!
retrieved.text: In the context of academic writing, it is the responsibility of the lecturer to determine the permitted use of aids. Any tools that are not explicitly permitted are prohibited. Prior to commencing work on your text, it is essential to familiarise yourself with the documentation requirements for the use of text-generating AI and other aids. It is imperative to take [data protection considerations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/#c1269046) into account at all times.

This also applies to Student Spaces and course rooms.

Students spend a lot of time in the digital realm, for example, to participate in courses, to research literature at the library or to interact with fellow students. The following information aims to contribute to your safety online.

© University of Vienna / derknopfdruecker.com

- [IT security: Tips for users](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/services/it-security/)
- [12 IT security commandments for users](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/it-news/article/news/12-it-security-gebote-fuer-nutzerinnen/?no_cache=1)
- [Password management](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/password/)
- [Tips regarding the password](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/password-tips/)

- [Social media and blog netiquette](https://blog.univie.ac.at/en/netiquette/)
- [How to prevent Zoombombing](https://zid.univie.ac.at/it-news/artikel/news/keine-angst-vor-zoombombing/) (in German)

Participating, asking questions, considering issues from different perspectives and interacting with others are central aspects of studying, but can also lead to conflict. You can contact the following organisational units to seek help for yourself or for all conflict parties, or to establish a network that can help you in case of problems.

© University of Vienna / derknopfdruecker.com

- [ÖH Helpline](https://www.oeh.ac.at/en/helpline/)
- [Psychological Counselling Service](https://www.studierendenberatung.at/en/)
- [Accessible Studying Team of the University of Vienna](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/accessible-studies/counselling/)
- [Sexual Harassment and Bullying Counselling Office](https://personalwesen.univie.ac.at/en/gender-equality-diversity/sexual-harassment-bullying-office/)
- [AKGleich - Equal Opportunities Working Party](https://gleichbehandlung.univie.ac.at/en/)
- [Arbiration Commission of the University of Vienna](https://schiedskommission.univie.ac.at/en/)

reranked.title: Things AI is not recommended for
heading_path: When it is the journey that matters
reranked.text:
There are tasks that require your personal attention to ensure genuine progress. This is particularly relevant in the context of academic writing, where the process of articulating ideas through writing often clarifies their intended expression. The writing process facilitates the organisation of ideas and their refinement into a coherent structure.

The ability to write is developed through practical experience. This process cannot be delegated to artificial intelligence. Mastering the writing process independently is not only essential, but also a fundamental learning outcome of academic work and a key requirement of good academic practice.

There are, however, ways to [support your writing process with AI](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/).

reranked.title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
reranked.text:
AI-supported tools can be customised to align with specific learning outcomes and work requirements. It is quite simple to configure a personalised AI tutor chatbot that supports academic work without compromising independent performance.

- Such an AI tutor can be set up to assume tasks at the linguistic and formal level, for example revising texts, correcting style or organising literature.
- With regard to substantive tasks, such as developing the line of argument, the tutor's role is to provide support and act as a partner in reflection, in the sense of a Socratic dialogue. By posing targeted questions, the tutor can stimulate critical thinking and in-depth engagement with the content, without undermining users' independence or creative contribution.

Configured like this, the AI tutor can offer targeted support without replacing intellectual work.

Recommendation: your AI tutor should first request that you define the learning outcomes of the assignment. On this basis, they will then be able to distinguish between [Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs)](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/#c1268678). If the learning outcomes are unclear, the tutor will assist in defining them. This ensures that AI is used in a targeted manner to provide support without undermining the independence required in academic work.

reranked.title: Basic framework for AI use
heading_path: Learning outcomes and independence
reranked.text: In degree programmes at the University of Vienna, courses and examinations pursue clearly defined learning outcomes. These are designed to develop subject-specific competences and to foster academic work. This includes understanding and accurately presenting content, applying knowledge to one’s own research questions and developing independent arguments, structuring texts coherently and using sources appropriately. The knowledge and skills required to achieve the learning outcomes must be developed independently. If AI assumes these tasks, the learning outcomes cannot be achieved. The competences associated with the learning outcomes are demonstrated in various course and examination formats, for example in examinations, presentations or written assignments. These competences must be demonstrated independently. This means that your work must reflect your own reasoning and learning processes. This also determines how AI may generally be used in degree programmes: - It may support learning processes, for example when structuring ideas or revising language. - However, it must not assume those competences that are to be assessed within the framework of a course or examination. Wie KI-Tools genutzt werden können, hängt also maßgeblich vom jeweiligen Lernziel ab, und diese können sich von Lehrveranstaltung zu Lehrveranstaltung unterscheiden. How AI tools may be used therefore depends to a significant extent on the respective learning outcome, which may vary from course to course. Example: - When translating a passage from French in an academic paper on a specific topic, it is

reranked.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
reranked.text:
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

reranked.title: Basic framework for AI use
heading_path: Learning outcomes and independence
reranked.text: tools may be used therefore depends to a significant extent on the respective learning outcome, which may vary from course to course. Example: - When translating a passage from French in an academic paper on a specific topic, it is important to engage with the concrete content. In this case, the use of an AI translation tool may be unproblematic, provided that the translation is checked for accuracy and the use of AI is disclosed. - In a language degree programme, translation itself is a central learning outcome. Therefore, if a translation task is delegated to an AI tool in this context, the independent performance expected of students would be undermined. If AI performs the work on your behalf, this contravenes the principles of academic integrity. Whether you are permitted to use artificial intelligence in your degree programme therefore primarily depends on whether the required learning outcomes and your independent contribution are preserved.

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
reranked.text: is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your studies, it is therefore important to use AI with restraint and to first develop your own learning strategies.

reranked.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Always follow the teacher's instructions!
reranked.text: In the context of academic writing, it is the responsibility of the lecturer to determine the permitted use of aids. Any tools that are not explicitly permitted are prohibited. Prior to commencing work on your text, it is essential to familiarise yourself with the documentation requirements for the use of text-generating AI and other aids. It is imperative to take [data protection considerations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/#c1269046) into account at all times.

selected.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
selected.text: permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

selected.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
selected.text:
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

selected.title: Basic framework for AI use
heading_path: Learning outcomes and independence
In short:
selected.text:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

selected.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
heading_path: This is usually prohibited:
selected.text:
The use of AI is prohibited in cases where it undermines the required competencies or where its use has not been disclosed. It is therefore prohibited to

- submit AI-generated texts or solutions as your own work
- allow AI to complete assignments, analyses or arguments in full or to a substantial extent
- use AI in examinations or other forms of assessment where this has not expressly been permitted
- adopt content generated by AI without indicating this where transparency is required
- use fabricated sources, quotations or bibliographies without verification

Such use may be regarded as a breach of the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) or as cheating.

selected.title: Prompting correctly
heading_path: Rule of Thumb: „Act as if it’s human“
Further tips
selected.text:
- Reduce complexity: If you give AI complex tasks, it may not perform them satisfactorily. In this case, it can help to break the task down into smaller parts and also tell the AI in which order the tasks should be performed:
  - Do X first, then Y
  - Proceed step by step
- Use different wording: Sometimes it helps to rephrase the prompt to make a difference.
- Check the scope of knowledge: AI systems are based on training data that was available up to a certain point in time. The AI you are using may not know anything about current events or specific topics (see Knowledge Cut-Off at [u:ai](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/)). If your prompt refers to something very new or niche knowledge, the AI may not have an answer. In this case, there is a high risk of [hallucinations](?id=225758#1268300).
- Take a break and try again: When a platform is heavily used or before or during technical updates, the systems sometimes do not respond as desired. In such cases, the best solution is often to take a break and try again later.
- Use online resources: Many AI platforms have helpful documentation or user forums where you can find tips and advice.

selected.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
selected.text: At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? The binding provisions specifying which AI tools are permitted for a particular course or examination can be found directly in the course description in u:find. As a general rule, anything not explicitly listed there as permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments,

[1] AI and Exams — Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

[2] What is permitted? And what is not?
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

[3] Basic framework for AI use — Learning outcomes and independence > In short:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

[4] What is permitted? And what is not? — This is usually prohibited:
The use of AI is prohibited in cases where it undermines the required competencies or where its use has not been disclosed. It is therefore prohibited to

- submit AI-generated texts or solutions as your own work
- allow AI to complete assignments, analyses or arguments in full or to a substantial extent
- use AI in examinations or other forms of assessment where this has not expressly been permitted
- adopt content generated by AI without indicating this where transparency is required
- use fabricated sources, quotations or bibliographies without verification

Such use may be regarded as a breach of the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) or as cheating.

[5] Prompting correctly — Rule of Thumb: „Act as if it’s human“ > Further tips
- Reduce complexity: If you give AI complex tasks, it may not perform them satisfactorily. In this case, it can help to break the task down into smaller parts and also tell the AI in which order the tasks should be performed:
  - Do X first, then Y
  - Proceed step by step
- Use different wording: Sometimes it helps to rephrase the prompt to make a difference.
- Check the scope of knowledge: AI systems are based on training data that was available up to a certain point in time. The AI you are using may not know anything about current events or specific topics (see Knowledge Cut-Off at [u:ai](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/)). If your prompt refers to something very new or niche knowledge, the AI may not have an answer. In this case, there is a high risk of [hallucinations](?id=225758#1268300).
- Take a break and try again: When a platform is heavily used or before or during technical updates, the systems sometimes do not respond as desired. In such cases, the best solution is often to take a break and try again later.
- Use online resources: Many AI platforms have helpful documentation or user forums where you can find tips and advice.

[6] AI and Exams — Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? The binding provisions specifying which AI tools are permitted for a particular course or examination can be found directly in the course description in u:find. As a general rule, anything not explicitly listed there as permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments,

prompt_messages.content:
You are a concise assistant for prospective and current University of Vienna students. Answer the question using ONLY the numbered sources provided.
Rules:
- Base every statement on the sources; never add outside knowledge or guess.
- Cite each claim inline with the bracket number(s) of the source(s) you used, e.g. [1] or [2][3]. Cite only sources you actually relied on.
- If the sources do not contain the answer, reply that you don't know, and do NOT cite any sources.
- Keep the answer short and direct (it may be read aloud by a voice assistant).

Question: asking hypothetically... if someone sneaks ai into an exam and gets caught, how bad is it — like just a fail or actually kicked out?

generate.raw_response: If someone uses unauthorized AI tools in an exam and gets caught, it can result in severe consequences, including a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded [1]. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters [1].

In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters [1].
